old_claim.note: Anatomical Studies (Vesalius, Detailed) is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: Vesalius-era detailed anatomy supplied the dissection-centered teaching subject matter that public anatomy theaters staged for students and spectators.

source_shape.source_locator: NLM presents Vesalius's De humani corporis fabrica as a 1543 dissection-based anatomical work with detailed woodcuts and dissection scenes, matching the anatomy curriculum later staged in public anatomy theaters.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source documents Vesalius-era detailed anatomy, dissection, and anatomical images as the scholarly subject matter for later anatomy teaching.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a contextual curriculum edge rather than a strict venue requirement, so the edge stays common_dependency with improved evidence.

ontology_before: Public Anatomy Theaters depended on Vesalius-style anatomical studies through an unsourced weak contextual edge.
ontology_after: Public Anatomy Theaters keep Vesalius-style anatomical studies as source-backed dissection-centered teaching context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: anatomical_studies_vesalius_detailed remains a direct common_dependency prerequisite with official-source evidence.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to seating architecture only, with no anatomy teaching or dissection curriculum.
A stronger source showed public anatomy theaters had no dependence on Vesalius-era detailed anatomy or dissection teaching.
The graph merged anatomical_studies_vesalius_detailed into human_anatomy_physiology and moved this edge to the broader node.
short_reason: Vesalius-era dissection anatomy is the teaching content staged by public anatomy theaters.
